items.correct: No — it was orchestrated by Goebbels and the Nazi Party using the assassination of a German diplomat as a pretext for coordinated state-sanctioned violence.
items.misconception: It was a spontaneous outburst of public anger against Jews.
items.random_wrong: Hitler personally led the attacks on synagogues.
items.why: Nazi propaganda framed it as spontaneous; documents prove it was organised. Students take the regime's framing at face value, missing how authoritarian violence is staged as popular will.

items.q: Why did the factory system replace the domestic system?
items.correct: Water-powered then steam-powered machinery was too large and expensive for cottages; factories concentrated workers around machines, enabling discipline, supervision, and scale.
items.misconception: Factories appeared because steam engines were invented.
items.random_wrong: Factories appeared because the government banned home weaving.
items.why: The shift was about machinery scale and capital investment, not just invention. Domestic spinning continued; factories triumphed where mechanisation outscaled hand work.

items.q: Why did Britain win the Battle of Britain?
items.correct: Radar, the Dowding command system, home advantage in pilot recovery, German tactical errors (switching to bombing cities), and adequate fighter production.
items.misconception: The RAF won because Spitfires were better than any German aircraft.
items.random_wrong: Hitler called off the attack to invade the USSR immediately.
items.why: The Bf 109 was comparable to the Spitfire. Victory came from integrated air defence and German strategic mistakes, not aircraft superiority — a key point in modern historiography.

items.q: What kind of castle did the Normans build first?
items.why: Correct answer: Motte-and-bailey. “Stone keep” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What was the Harrying of the North?
items.why: It's William's brutal punishment of rebels. “A peace deal” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was Domesday Book completed?
items.why: Exact answer: 1086. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
